figures.caption: TABLE III: Coverage Metrics for Different Designs with Varying Number of Selected Signals
TABLE IV: Beating Baselines with Minimal Assertions for Superior Coverage Metrics via Stepwise Signal Addition

Fig. 2: The workflow of the AgileAssert framework. Our RTL parser constructs a semantic graph from the RTL code. Signals are then scored using multiple features and filtered through selection mechanism to produce a ranked list of signals. These signals guide structure-aware RTL slicing, which in turn directs LLM-based targeted assertion generation.
Fig. 6: Progressive error detection rate improvement via stepwise signal addition compared to baseline methods.
Fig. 7: COI coverage vs. input token consumption across benchmarks.
Fig. 3: Example of a simplified RTL semantic graph from an I2C circuit [10]. The design consists of three modules.
Fig. 4: Example prompt for targeted assertion generation based on RTL slices of the c_state signal in the i2c_master_byte_ctrl module.
Fig. 5: Signal-centric RTL slicing and prompt-driven assertion generation: an example with the c_state signal in the I2C design.
